---
title: "AI Contract Ladder"
---

This lesson turns the AI application lane into a sequence of proof-carrying contracts. The point is not to claim that circular structure improves every model. The point is to learn how a real ML component can expose a finite circular boundary, attach theorem ids to that boundary, and keep performance claims separate from proof claims.

::: {.lesson-goal}

## Goal

Read the Circle AI contracts in dependency order: exact phase banks, ring-buffer freshness, sparse-attention coverage, and circulant mixing. By the end, you should be able to name the circular address space, the repeated action, the Lean-backed statement, the executable sidecar, and the claim boundary for each contract.

:::

## Contract Pattern

Every usable contract in this lane has the same shape:

```text
engineering object
  -> finite circular address space
  -> repeated action or stride
  -> theorem-backed boundary
  -> Python/browser certificate
  -> explicit non-claim
```

The non-claim matters. Theorem cards certify structural facts such as collision, coverage, freshness, equivariance, or exact budget accounting. They do not certify model quality, speed, memory savings, or production safety.

::: {.warning-box}
The browser widgets, Python CLIs, benchmarks, and fixtures on this page are executable references and not proofs. This page does not claim model-quality, speed, memory, or real-data usefulness results; theorem cards and Lean declarations remain the proof source of truth.
:::

## Ladder

| Step | Component | Circular Object | What Gets Certified | Main Lesson |
| ---: | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | RoPE phase-bank certifier | residues modulo declared periods | exact discrete position collision and distinguishability | proof-carrying phase contracts |
| 2 | Real-phase RoPE seed | turn ratio below a finite context | theorem-backed one-channel finite margin examples | numerical diagnostics need proof-backed bounds |
| 3 | KV-cache ring buffer | token slots modulo cache size | slot collision, retained-window membership, trace-fresh batch slot distinctness, live-window duplicate freedom | freshness is residue plus time boundary |
| 4 | Sparse attention | local windows plus stride-family lag candidates | covered lags, gap witnesses, hit/gap list iffs, budget caps, no-duplicate candidate survival | sparse plans need hit and gap certificates, not just examples |
| 5 | Looped recurrence | loop phase and finite recurrence budgets | budget closure, exit certificates, active-token boundaries, route bounds | recursive-transformer ideas need schedule contracts before quality claims |
| 6 | Circulant mixers | cyclic shift on token indices | shift equivariance, convolution laws, linearity | structure can be guaranteed before benchmarking |

## Hands-On Sequence

Read and run the contracts in this order:

1. **RoPE certifier:** start with a position-distinguishability contract, where pass/fail is easiest to see.
2. **KV-cache ring buffer:** add the time boundary that distinguishes a live residue from a stale same-slot token.
3. **Sparse-attention coverage:** inspect a plan that intentionally has both covered lags and theorem-backed gap witnesses.

After those three, the later recurrence and mixer lessons should read as variations on the same pattern: identify the finite circular object, name the repeated action, expose a failure mode, and keep empirical model claims separate.

## 1. Exact RoPE Phase Banks

The exact certifier models a declared phase bank as integer periods. Two positions collide in one channel exactly when the period divides their ordered gap, and they collide in the whole bank exactly when every declared period divides that same gap.

This is the cleanest contract because it gives both pass and fail evidence: a context either fits before the common collision gap, or the certifier can show sample colliding pairs.

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0021"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0024"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0026"></div>

::: {.warning-box}
This is exact for the declared integer-period phase-bank model. It is not a claim that real-valued RoPE channels have exact integer periods.
:::

## 2. Real-Phase RoPE Seeds

Real RoPE moves from divisibility to distance from an integer turn. The current theorem program proves finite-margin certificates for named ratios and a bounded standard channel-0 seed. That is the important shift: the question becomes quantitative, and the proof has to account for every inspected positive gap.

The longest current proved standard seed is a one-channel statement for the standard channel-0 turn ratio:

```text
turn ratio = 1 / (2*pi)
context    = 16384
margin     = 1 / 104219
```

The conditional bank bridge says that if a finite real-phase bank contains that channel and the inspected context and margin are within a seed, then the all-channel near-turn condition is ruled out under the stated tolerance scaling. The concrete D12, D13, and D14 bridges package the common bank shape where standard channel 0 is first and additional frequencies follow. The theorem trail also proves useful negative companions: gap `710` blocks the doubled D9 margin `1/65536`, the nearby D10 margin `1/104000`, and every advertised margin at or above `1/104218`. The bracket theorems package the useful engineering reading: at contexts `4096`, `8192`, and `16384`, a nearby positive margin is proved, while `1/104218` and larger advertised margins are impossible for this channel. The sidecar now emits a `standard_channel0_frontier_summary`: 16k is theorem-backed, 32k and 64k are exact-rational candidate rows, and the larger request first fails at gap `103993`; those candidate rows are not theorem boxes until generated rational-band certificates are checked. `AIRA-T0139` and `AIRA-T0140` are the proved compression bridge for that next certificate route.

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0061"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0101"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0102"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0103"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0104"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0105"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0108"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0109"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0110"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0111"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0114"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0115"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0116"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0117"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0118"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0119"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0120"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0121"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0122"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0123"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0124"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0125"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0126"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0127"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0128"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0129"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0130"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0131"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0132"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0133"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0134"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0135"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0136"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0137"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0138"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0139"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0140"></div>

::: {.warning-box}
This is not a full real-RoPE bank theorem. It is a theorem-backed channel-0 seed plus a one-separating-channel conditional bank transfer. The remaining hard work is broader channel-wise certification or sharper Diophantine machinery.
:::

## 3. KV-Cache Ring Buffer

A fixed-size KV cache writes token `t` to slot `t mod cache_size`. The slot residue alone cannot prove freshness, because stale and live tokens can share a slot across time. The useful contract is the retained-window boundary:

```text
token is retained at current
iff
token <= current and current < token + cache_size
```

The generated live-window theorem then upgrades a Python-style list of live tokens into a theorem-backed object: membership in the list is equivalent to retention, the whole live window maps to duplicate-free slots, and a full live window has one in-range emitted entry per declared cache slot. The read/write guard says that a retained token has no later same-slot writer before the current read point, the stale-witness theorem gives `token + cache_size` as the concrete later same-slot write for stale non-future tokens, the trace iff says those two sides exactly characterize freshness, and the batch trace theorem lifts the same check to a declared read batch.

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0069"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0070"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0073"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0074"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0075"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0076"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0077"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0078"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0079"></div>

## 4. Sparse-Attention Coverage

A strided sparse-attention path is a coil orbit on a finite context. The basic theorem says a stride reaches every position iff it is coprime to the context size. The useful engineering contract is stronger: for a local window plus a finite family of stride paths, the certifier should report covered lags, uncovered-lag witnesses, and candidate-budget boundaries.

This is why the sparse lane now emphasizes iffs and gap certificates. A sparse plan is more useful when it can tell you what it misses.

The sparse contract now has both sides of the story: the default `C_120` fixture reports `109` uncovered positive lags, while the compact `C_9` fixture with local window `2`, path length `2`, and strides `(3,4,7)` has an empty uncovered-lag list and preserves the raw lag/query candidate budget.

[Open the guided sparse-attention coverage lesson](sparse_attention_contract.qmd).

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0002"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0020"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0078"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0079"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0080"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0081"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0083"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0084"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0085"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0086"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0087"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0088"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0089"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0090"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0091"></div>

## 5. Looped Recurrence

Looped and recursive transformer proposals are easy to overstate because "more computation" can sound like "more reasoning." The contract form keeps the finite part separate: loop budgets, exit certificates, active-token boundaries, selected-block routes, and wrong-period controls.

[Open the guided looped recurrence contracts lesson](looped_recurrence_contracts.qmd).

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0018"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0033"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0035"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0039"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0049"></div>

## 6. Circulant Mixers

A circulant mixer is not a proof that a model will perform well. It is a structure with a guarantee: cyclic shifts commute with the mixer, and the convolution laws are fixed before training. That makes it a good negative-control example for the whole AI lane: the proof can certify equivariance, while benchmarks decide whether that bias fits the data.

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0006"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0007"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0008"></div>

## How To Use This Ladder

For a new AI component, fill in this checklist before writing a theorem or benchmark:

1. What is the finite circular address space?
2. What operation repeats, wraps, rotates, or strides?
3. What exact failure mode should the contract expose?
4. Which statement needs an iff, counterexample witness, count, or quantitative bound?
5. What Python/browser certificate would a non-Lean engineer run?
6. Which claim is deliberately outside the theorem?

If the answer is only "this has a circle metaphor," it is not ready. If the answer produces a pass/fail certificate, a gap witness, a collision count, or a margin bound, it belongs in this lane.

## Source Trail

<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_04_ROPE_POSITION_CERTIFIER.md">RoPE paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_04_ROPE_POSITION_CERTIFIER.md">Proof-Carrying RoPE Position Distinguishability</a></p>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY.md">Attention and memory paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY.md">Coil Attention And Memory</a></p>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_01_CIRCLE_AI_ARCHITECTURES.md">Architecture paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_01_CIRCLE_AI_ARCHITECTURES.md">Circle AI Architectures</a></p>

## Dictionary

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0028"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0076"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0077"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0079"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0081"></div>

<div class="book-navigation">
<a href="ai.qmd">Previous: AI</a>
<a href="rope_certifier.qmd">Next: RoPE Certifier</a>
</div>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
